title: Be vs Go: Location, State and Movement
learningObjectives: Understand when to use 'be' for location, states and identity
Understand when to use 'go' for movement and activities

sections.title: Why Be and Go Are Confusing
sections.content: Many Spanish speakers confuse <be> and <go> because in Spanish, <estar> and <ir> work differently. In English, <be> describes WHERE you are or HOW you are, while <go> describes movement FROM one place TO another. This lesson will help you understand the difference clearly.

sections.title: Be for Location (Where You Are)
sections.content:
Use <be> to say WHERE someone or something is right now. You are NOT moving — you are already there.

• <I am at home.> — Estoy en casa (ya estoy ahí)
• <She is in the office.> — Ella está en la oficina
• <The book is on the table.> — El libro está en la mesa
• <They are at school.> — Ellos están en la escuela

Key prepositions with <be>: at, in, on, near, next to, between.

sections.title: Be for States and Feelings
sections.content:
Use <be> to describe HOW someone feels or what state they are in. No movement is involved.

• <I am tired.> — Estoy cansado
• <She is happy.> — Ella está feliz
• <We are ready.> — Estamos listos
• <It is cold today.> — Hace frío hoy
• <He is a teacher.> — Él es profesor (identity)
• <The party is at 8pm.> — La fiesta es a las 8pm (schedule)

Remember: <be> = no movement, just describing a situation.

sections.title: Go for Movement (Where You Are Going)
sections.content:
Use <go> when someone MOVES from one place to another. There is direction or destination.

• <I go home.> — Voy a casa (me muevo hacia casa)
• <She goes to the office.> — Ella va a la oficina
• <They go to school every day.> — Van a la escuela todos los días
• <The bus goes to the station.> — El autobús va a la estación

Key prepositions with <go>: to, towards, back to, out, away.

sections.title: Go + -ing for Activities
sections.content:
A special pattern: use <go + verb-ing> for sports and leisure activities.

• <I go swimming every day.> — Voy a nadar todos los días
• <He goes running in the morning.> — Él va a correr por la mañana
• <We go shopping on weekends.> — Vamos de compras los fines de semana
• <They go hiking in summer.> — Van de excursión en verano

Common: go swimming, go running, go shopping, go fishing, go camping, go dancing.

sections.title: Be vs Go Side by Side
sections.content:
Example 1: LOCATION vs MOVEMENT
<I am at the park.> (I'm already there) vs <I go to the park.> (I'm moving there)

Example 2: STATE vs ACTION
<She is tired.> (describing her state) vs <She goes to bed.> (movement to bed)

Example 3: PRESENCE vs ROUTINE
<We are at the gym.> (we're there now) vs <We go to the gym every Monday.> (regular movement)

Example 4: COMMON MISTAKE
❌ <I go at home.> → ✓ <I am at home.> (location, no movement)
❌ <I am to school every day.> → ✓ <I go to school every day.> (movement)

sections.title: Quick Reference
sections.content:
Use <BE> when: you describe WHERE something is (location), HOW someone feels (state), WHAT someone is (identity), or WHEN something happens (time/schedule).

Use <GO> when: someone MOVES to a place (movement), someone does an activity (<go + -ing>), or you talk about regular trips (routine movement).

Simple test: Is there movement? → Use <go>. No movement? → Use <be>.

keyVocabulary.definition: Used for location, states, identity, and descriptions
keyVocabulary.example: I am at home. She is happy.

keyVocabulary.definition: Used for movement towards a place or doing activities
keyVocabulary.example: I go to school. He goes running.

keyVocabulary.definition: Preposition used with 'be' for specific locations

grammarPoints.explanation: Use <be> (am/is/are) when there is NO movement. You describe where something IS or how someone FEELS.
examples: I am at home. (location — already there)
commonMistakes: "I am to school" ❌ → "I am at school" ✓ (use 'at' not 'to' with be)
"She is to the office" ❌ → "She is in the office" ✓

grammarPoints.explanation: Use <go> (go/goes) when there IS movement from one place to another, or for activities with go + -ing.
examples: I go to school every day. (movement)
He goes running in the park. (activity)
commonMistakes: "I go at home" ❌ → "I go home" ✓ (no preposition with 'home' after go)
"I go to swim" ❌ → "I go swimming" ✓ (use go + -ing for activities)
